findings.title: Hardcoded AWS access key in test fixture
findings.evidence: New file adds `export const fakeAwsKey = "AKIA0123456789ABCDEF";` — an AWS-format access key ID (AKIA prefix) hardcoded and exported in source. The diff confirms this is the sole added line. Even in a test fixture, an exported AKIA-pattern credential is a trust risk that should be moved to an env var or secret store; the AKIA prefix is also a real-secret pattern that triggers scanners.

notes: The value appears to be a placeholder/fake AWS key (sequential 0123456789ABCDEF body, variable named fakeAwsKey), so the exposure may be lower-impact than a live credential; however the prompt title states it should still be flagged, and the AKIA-format export remains the kind of pattern that should be kept out of source.
Diff Drift correctly identified the single changed file and the hardcoded-secret flag; the raw diff matches the report exactly (one added line, new file).
Only one file changed and it was analyzed (no skipped/oversized files).

evaluator.note: Benchmark v4 blind run; re-run for this case after the engine change that flags secrets in test files (packet content changed). Packet-only context.
